title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an archaeologist who studies how ordinary people lived in ancient Rome. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Lives in the Ruins
content.subtitle: A magazine article about an archaeologist devoted to uncovering the daily lives of ancient Rome's ordinary citizens.
content.text: Most people, when they think of ancient Rome, think of emperors. They think of the Colosseum, of marble statues, of military conquest spreading across continents. Dr Fabia Rossi thinks about laundry. [31] More precisely, she thinks about the commercial laundries — known as fullonicae — that operated throughout the Roman city, and about the workers who spent their days treading cloth in large vats of human urine, which was the standard cleaning agent of the ancient world. [/31] It is not a glamorous subject. It is, she would argue, a far more important one. || Dr Rossi is part of a generation of classical archaeologists who have shifted their attention away from the monuments and toward the mundane. Her research, based primarily at sites in Ostia and Pompeii, focuses on the material evidence of everyday commercial life: the traces of workshops, market stalls, and small businesses that together constituted the economic backbone of Roman urban existence. 'The emperors built the aqueducts and the forums,' she told me, 'but someone had to bake the bread. Thousands of people, every day, keeping a city of a million people alive. [32] Those are the people I want to understand.' [/32] || The challenge she faces is that the ancient sources — the texts that have survived — were almost exclusively written by and for the wealthy and educated. The fullers who cleaned Rome's togas, the bakers, the street food vendors: none of them wrote histories. Their lives must be reconstructed from physical evidence, graffiti, legal documents, and the occasional passing reference in the works of writers who considered such people barely worth mentioning. [33] Dr Rossi has spent her career developing methods to make these silences speak — to extract social and economic information from sources that historians of an earlier generation would have dismissed as trivial. [/33] || One of her recent projects involved a systematic analysis of oven remains at a bakery site in Ostia. By examining the residues left behind in the ovens and combining this data with evidence from the building's layout, she was able to reconstruct the likely output and working patterns of the bakery — suggesting, among other things, that it operated two shifts of workers and supplied bread to a network of outlets across the neighbourhood. [34] What is remarkable about this finding is not the detail itself but the method: archaeology that can trace the rhythms of a working day across a gap of two thousand years. [/34] || Dr Rossi is careful not to romanticise her subjects. Life for Rome's working poor was hard by any measure — precarious, physically punishing, and largely unprotected by law. [35] But she resists the tendency of some popular histories to present ancient workers as simply pitiable. 'They were skilled,' she says firmly. 'They organised. They formed professional associations. They had opinions and preferences and aspirations. They were people, not props.' [/35] || When I ask what keeps her returning to these particular lives, she answers without hesitation. [36] 'Because they were here,' she says. 'Because they built the place, and history forgot them, and someone should pay attention.' [/36]

questions.question: The writer opens by contrasting the popular image of ancient Rome with Dr Rossi's subject in order to
options: A) suggest that Dr Rossi's research is less important than traditional classical studies.
B) create an amusing contrast between grand history and an unglamorous reality.
C) argue that Roman laundry workers were more important than Roman emperors.
D) explain why Dr Rossi chose to specialise in commercial rather than political history.
questions.explanation: The contrast between emperors, the Colosseum and marble — and then 'Dr Rossi thinks about laundry' — creates a deliberate comic contrast before the article explains why her subject matters.

questions.question: Dr Rossi's comment about bakers and city workers suggests that she believes
options: A) the Roman economy was more sophisticated than historians generally acknowledge.
B) ordinary workers deserve as much historical attention as powerful figures.
C) the emperors were less important to Rome's survival than they are given credit for.
D) Roman workers were treated unfairly in comparison with workers in other ancient cultures.
questions.explanation: She acknowledges the emperors' role but then identifies 'those are the people I want to understand' — the workers — clearly valuing them as historical subjects in their own right.

questions.question: The phrase 'make these silences speak' refers to Dr Rossi's ability to
options: A) find previously unknown ancient texts written by working-class Romans.
B) persuade historians to give more attention to non-literary sources.
C) recover meaningful information from sources previously considered unimportant.
D) translate ancient graffiti and legal documents from Latin into modern languages.
questions.explanation: The 'silences' are the gaps left by groups who left no written records; Dr Rossi's method is to extract information from physical evidence and minor sources 'that historians of an earlier generation would have dismissed as trivial'.

questions.question: The writer describes Dr Rossi's analysis of the bakery as 'remarkable' because
options: A) no previous researcher had thought to examine food production at Roman sites.
B) the technique allows detailed knowledge of daily working life to be recovered across centuries.
C) the bakery was operating with a level of industrial organisation not previously known in Rome.
D) the findings revealed that Roman bakers were among the city's wealthiest citizens.
questions.explanation: The writer says what is remarkable is 'not the detail itself but the method: archaeology that can trace the rhythms of a working day across a gap of two thousand years' — the method's reach across time is what impresses.

questions.question: Dr Rossi's statement that Roman workers 'were people, not props' is a criticism of
options: A) historians who argue that Rome's working classes had no political influence.
B) popular accounts that reduce ancient workers to helpless victims.
C) academics who focus only on the legal status of workers in ancient society.
D) films and TV programmes that portray ancient Romans inaccurately.
questions.explanation: Dr Rossi says she 'resists the tendency of some popular histories to present ancient workers as simply pitiable', and her claim they were skilled, organised, and ambitious counters the 'props' — passive background figures — framing.

questions.question: Dr Rossi's final comment reveals that her fundamental motivation is
options: A) professional ambition to establish a new field within classical archaeology.
B) a personal commitment to giving historical recognition to those who were forgotten.
C) frustration with a discipline that has traditionally ignored non-elite Roman citizens.
D) a desire to prove that material evidence is more reliable than ancient written sources.
questions.explanation: Her answer focuses on the workers themselves — 'they built the place and history forgot them' — and the personal obligation she feels: 'someone should pay attention'. This is about recognition, not professional ambition.
